reasoning_model_1: The model generates the full text accurately without errors. Regarding the paralinguistic requirements, the audio effectively splits the performance. From 0:00 to 0:05, the speaker uses a laid-back, slightly dragging, and disinterested tone to deliver 'This group project, we can just do it casually, right?', which aligns well with the 'perfunctory' instruction. At 0:05, there is a clear transition marked by the interjection 'Huh'. From 0:06 to the end, the pitch drops slightly and the pace becomes more deliberate and emphatic, conveying the required 'sincere' tone for 'For the honor of our group, we must take it seriously!'. The contrast is distinct and natural.
reasoning_model_2: The model fails significantly on content accuracy. It begins with a hallucinated conversational filler 'Got it' which is not in the script. Furthermore, the audio cuts off abruptly after the first sentence ('...we can just do it casually, right?'), followed by a laugh. It omits the entire second half of the text ('Huh, what you said makes a lot of sense! For the honor of our group, we must take it seriously!'). Because the second half is missing, the model completely fails to demonstrate the required switch to a sincere tone.
model_comparison: Model 1 followed all instructions perfectly, delivering the full text with the requested dynamic tone shift from perfunctory to sincere. The difference in attitude between the two halves of the audio was clear and natural. Model 2 failed on the most basic level of content accuracy; it included hallucinated conversational fillers and truncated the audio halfway through, missing the pivotal tone shift entirely. Therefore, Model 1 is the clear winner.
